evals.prompt: Our subscription brand has been flat at 12k active subscribers for three months. Acquisition feels fine but we can't grow. Help.
evals.expected_output: Diagnosis grounded in the net-subscriber-growth decomposition. The response should pull the subscriber growth equation inputs, separate trial-driven adds from direct signups, compare gross adds against voluntary + involuntary churn, and flag whether the issue is acquisition leak or churn leak. If reactivation potential is meaningful, recommend it as the first move before touching net-new acquisition.
assertions: Checks for .agents/modern-ai-context.md
Calls modern.subscriptions.active_count via modern-mcp if connected
Calls modern.subscriptions.churn_rate split by voluntary and involuntary
Applies the net-new vs reactivation framework decomposition
Flags whether the bottleneck is gross adds or churn
Recommends reactivation flow as a candidate first move
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections
Names a single highest-leverage next move

evals.prompt: Trial-to-paid conversion is 32%. Is that bad? How do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Diagnosis that 32% is below the typical 40-60% band, then walks through the four levers of trial-to-paid mechanics — activation depth, reminder cadence, trial length, trial friction — in order. Should ask about activation rate and reminder cadence before recommending discount tactics. Should not jump to discount.
assertions: Identifies 32% as below the typical 40-60% band
Walks through the four levers of trial-to-paid mechanics in order
Asks about activation rate before recommending discount tactics
Recommends trial-end reminder cadence (7/3/1 days) as a Quick Win
Does not lead with a discount as the primary fix
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Should I push annual plans? Right now everyone signs up monthly.
evals.expected_output: A targeted answer that uses the annual-vs-monthly framework. Should explain that annual at signup is usually the wrong placement and recommend a month-2 or month-3 'switch to annual' offer instead. Should walk through the cash-flow impact of converting a monthly subscriber to annual and connect it to paid CAC payback economics.
assertions: Recommends against annual at signup unless trial conversion is broken
Recommends a month-2 or month-3 switch-to-annual offer placement
Walks through the cash-flow impact of annual conversion
Names the 17% conventional discount band
Connects annual placement to CAC payback economics
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our onboarding sequence is two welcome emails. Should we do more?
evals.expected_output: Diagnosis that onboarding is about activation depth, not email count. Should ask what the activation moment is for the product (first shipment consumed, first session completed, etc.), then design the onboarding sequence to drive customers to that moment. Should distinguish reminder cadence from activation prompts.
assertions: Defines activation moment first, then designs around it
Distinguishes activation prompts from reminder cadence
Recommends measuring activation rate as the leading indicator
Does not just recommend 'more emails'
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: I'm 4 months in with ~800 active subscribers on my new supplement brand. Where should I focus?
evals.expected_output: Activates the founding-customer cohort acceleration framework because the brand is under 12 months and under 1k subscribers. Should recommend founders-only pricing preservation, direct founder communication, and beta access tactics. Should also walk through whether net-new acquisition or activation deepening matters more at this scale.
assertions: Activates the founding-cohort framework explicitly
Recommends founders-only pricing preservation
Recommends direct founder communication tactics
Discusses beta product access as a retention lever
Notes that the framework changes above ~5k subscribers
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: I want to redesign my subscription landing page. What matters most for conversion?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the five subscription-specific CRO levers in order of impact — cancellation transparency, shipment cadence preview, pause option visibility, social proof shape, first-shipment composition. Should distinguish these from generic CRO tactics. Should ask about current cancellation language and pause availability before recommending tests.
assertions: Lists the five subscription-specific CRO levers in order of impact
Distinguishes from generic CRO tactics
Asks about current cancellation transparency and pause option
Recommends cancellation transparency near CTA as a Quick Win
Recommends shipment cadence preview for replenishment brands
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our paid channel CAC is $48 and our average subscription value is $32/month. Is that working?
evals.expected_output: Subscription payback math: at $32/month and 65-70% retention at month 3, payback is approximately 2-3 months depending on churn. Should explicitly compute months-to-payback factoring in churn timing, then place the channel in the LTV decision framework. Should reference paid-ltv-optimization for the deeper channel-by-channel analysis.
assertions: Computes months-to-payback factoring in churn timing
Does not just do CAC / monthly value math
Distinguishes subscription payback from one-time-purchase payback
References paid-ltv-optimization for deeper channel analysis
Calls modern.ads.cac_by_channel filtered to subscription_first_purchase
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections
